profiles.notes: Fail-safe profile. Applied whenever location is unknown, stale, low-confidence, permission-denied, or unmatched by any rule. Never loosen this automatically.

Jurisdictions where wearable recording of public space is not lawfully available to private individuals. Blurring does not cure this — the restriction attaches to the act of recording, not to data retention. Not user-overridable; admin config only.

Caméras-piétons are a regulated regime under the Code de la sécurité intérieure, available to police, gendarmerie, municipal agents and authorised private security (prefectural authorisation + CNIL conformity). Private personal use is not provided for. CNIL: only public authorities may film the public way. Blur does not cure it.

profiles.noticeText: Grabación en curso. Los rostros se difuminan automáticamente.
profiles.notes: AEPD: capturing/recording the vía pública for security purposes is reserved to State security forces; private capture only where strictly indispensable or unavoidable. Domestic exemption has been accepted for personal-memory recording (e.g. helmet cam of one's own trips) but is doubtful once the purpose is security/evidence. Blur-at-capture only — retained raw would be pseudonymised and remain personal data (EDPB Guidelines 01/2025). AEPD retention max 30 days.

PDPA does not apply to individuals acting in a personal or domestic capacity. Outward-facing footage of public space is also likely publicly-available data. Most permissive of the researched set. EXEMPTION LOST if devices are issued by an organisation to staff — then full PDPA consent/notification duties apply. Voyeurism and harassment offences still apply independently.

PDPO turns on identifiability: PCPD holds that where identity cannot be ascertained, footage is not collection of personal data and falls outside the Ordinance — so blurring has unusually high legal value here. Eastweek: photographing an anonymous subject is not collection. BUT retaining raw for later identification pulls it back into the PDPO and the six DPPs (purpose, proportionality, retention, security). Publishing hard-blocked: PDPO doxxing offence covers disclosure intended or reckless as to causing specified harm. PCPD Dec-2025 guidance on CCTV/in-vehicle/drone cameras expects necessity, proportionality, notice and encryption at rest and in transit.

New LFPDPPP (DOF 20 Mar 2025, in force 21 Mar 2025) Art. 1(II) exempts collection/storage for exclusively personal use, WITHOUT disclosure or commercial use. The words 'sin fines de divulgación' are load-bearing: the exemption collapses the moment footage is shared. publishingAllowed:false is therefore protecting the exemption itself, not just avoiding a separate offence — dissemination without consent (incl. WhatsApp/social media) is penalised. Footage may still be offered as evidence in a denuncia to the Ministerio Público — that route is expressly contemplated and should not be treated as 'publishing'. State-level video rules may add requirements.

QLD/NT: recording a private conversation you are a party to is permitted without other parties' consent. Federal Privacy Act excludes acts of individuals in a personal capacity. Surveillance Devices Acts target 'private activity', not public filming. Statutory tort of serious invasion of privacy (Schedule 2, Privacy Act 1988, commenced 10 Jun 2025) reaches individuals, covers intrusion upon seclusion, is actionable without proof of damage — capture in public is not automatically safe from it. Publication of recorded private conversations is separately restricted.

VIC/NSW/SA/WA/TAS/ACT: private conversations generally require all-party consent, so audio is off by default and the audio incident trigger is unavailable — rely on IMU, animal-approach and the manual button. A 'lawful interests' exception exists in several states but is fact-specific and must not be assumed by the app. Video in public remains permitted. Vic/WA additionally restrict optical recording of a 'private activity' which can extend to conduct inside a building even where publicly accessible. Statutory tort (10 Jun 2025) applies.

rules.notes: Deliberately the stricter profile — the bare-country fallback must not assume one-party consent.
